title: Inflation & deflation
summary:
## The big idea Inflation means your money buys less every year. A 10% inflation rate turns \$100 of groceries into \$110 — same trolley, more money. Understanding what drives the **general price level**, how we measure it, and who wins and loses when it moves, is the heart of AS macro. Get the definitions razor-sharp first: - **Inflation** — a **sustained rise in the general price level** (equivalently: a fall in the purchasing power of money). Not one product getting dearer, and not a one-off jump — *sustained* and *general*. - **Deflation** — a sustained **fall** in the general price level. - **Disinflation** — inflation **slowing down** (from 8% to 3%, say). Prices are still *rising* — just less quickly. Confusing this with deflation is the classic trap. ## Measuring inflation — the Consumer Price Index (CPI) Statisticians can't track every price, so they track a representative **basket**: 1. A household expenditure survey finds what people actually buy. 2. Each item gets a **weight** matching its share of spending — a 10% rise in rent matters far more than a 10% rise in the price of matches. 3. Prices are collected monthly across the country. 4. The weighted average becomes an **index** (base year = 100); the **% change in the index** over a year is the inflation rate. If the CPI goes from 100 to 106, inflation is 6%; if it then reaches 110.2, the next year's rate is (110.2 − 106) ÷ 106 ≈ 4% — disinflation. **Limitations:** the basket dates quickly (new products, quality improvements); it is a national *average* that matches nobody exactly (a pensioner's basket ≠ a student's); and changes in weights and methods make long comparisons tricky. ## Causes of inflation **Demand-pull inflation** — total spending grows faster than the economy's ability to produce, especially near full capacity: 'too much money chasing too few goods'. On the diagram, **AD shifts right** along a steep AS. Typical triggers: consumer credit booms, big fiscal stimulus, export surges. **Cost-push inflation** — production costs rise and firms pass them on. **SRAS shifts left**: the price level rises *while output falls*. Typical triggers: energy and raw-material price shocks, wage rises above productivity, a **depreciating currency** (dearer imports), higher indirect taxes.
**What each part means:** the economy starts at price level **P1**, output **Y1**. A cost shock — dearer oil, higher wages, a weaker currency — shifts short-run aggregate supply from **SRAS1** left/up to the dashed **SRAS2**. At the new equilibrium (orange dot) the price level has **risen to P2** while real GDP has **fallen to Y2**. Prices up *and* output down together is the signature of cost-push — in its severe form, **stagflation**. (For demand-pull, AD shifts right instead: prices *and* output rise.)
A third lens: the **monetarist** view that inflation ultimately comes from the **money supply** growing faster than output — 'inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon' (Friedman). Rapid money growth financed by printing is the road to **hyperinflation** (Zimbabwe 2008, Venezuela 2018). ## Consequences of inflation - **Purchasing power falls** — hardest on those with **fixed incomes** (pensioners) and workers with weak bargaining power. - **Savers and lenders lose, borrowers gain** — if inflation beats the interest rate, the *real* value of savings and debts shrinks. This quietly **redistributes** wealth. - **Loss of competitiveness** — if our inflation outpaces trading partners', exports get relatively dear → current account worsens. - **Menu costs** (reprinting price lists) and **shoe-leather costs** (economising on cash holdings). - **Uncertainty** — firms can't judge future costs and prices, so investment falls. **Fiscal drag** pulls pay rises into higher tax bands. - Perspective: **low, stable inflation (~2%)** is the actual target, not zero — it greases relative price and wage adjustments and keeps a safe distance from deflation. ## Why deflation can be worse Falling prices sound lovely and are usually poison. If prices are expected to keep falling, consumers **postpone purchases** (why buy today what's cheaper next month?) → AD falls → firms cut output, wages and jobs → prices fall further: a **deflationary spiral**. Meanwhile the **real burden of debt rises** — incomes fall but repayments don't. Japan spent two decades fighting exactly this. (Distinguish this 'bad' *demand-side* deflation from 'benign' deflation caused by technology cutting costs.) ## Why it matters Diagnosis drives policy: demand-pull calls for cooling demand (Unit 5's fiscal/monetary tools); cost-push calls for supply-side fixes — squeezing demand against an oil shock just deepens the slump. Expect data questions asking you to *identify the type* of inflation from clues.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Inflation** = sustained rise in the *general* price level; **deflation** = sustained fall; **disinflation** = slower inflation (prices still rising). - **CPI**: survey → weights → priced basket → index; rate = % change. Limits: outdated basket, average hides differences. - **Demand-pull**: AD→right near capacity ('too much money chasing too few goods'). - **Cost-push**: SRAS→left (energy, wages, weak currency, indirect taxes) → prices↑, output↓ (stagflation). - Monetarist: excess **money-supply growth** → hyperinflation risk. - Losers: fixed incomes, savers, lenders, exporters. Winners: borrowers. - **Deflation danger**: delayed spending + rising real debt → spiral. Target ≈ **2%**, not 0%.

keyTerms.definition: A sustained rise in the general price level, reducing money's purchasing power.
A sustained fall in the general price level.
A fall in the rate of inflation; prices still rise but more slowly.
A weighted index of the price of a representative basket of goods and services.
Inflation caused by AD rising faster than supply near full capacity.
Inflation caused by rising costs shifting SRAS to the left.
The quantity of goods and services a unit of money can buy.
Falling prices → delayed spending → falling AD, output and wages → further falls.

diagrams: **Demand-pull:** AD shifts right along a steep/near-vertical AS → higher price level and output.
**Cost-push:** SRAS shifts left → higher price level, lower output (stagflation).
Optional: a diagram of the **CPI basket/weights** concept, or a price-index line over time.
tips: Define inflation precisely as a **sustained rise in the *general* price level** — not a one-off or a single good's price.
Always diagnose the **type** (demand-pull vs cost-push) with the correct AD/AS shift — it drives the policy answer.
Distinguish **disinflation** (rate falling) from **deflation** (prices falling) — a common trap.
Give **CPI limitations** (outdated basket, average masks differences) for evaluation.
Explain why **deflation can be worse than mild inflation** (delayed spending, rising real debt) — a strong higher-mark point.

channels.name: tutor2u Economics
channels.why: Clear on inflation causes, CPI and consequences.

revisionNotes:
# Inflation & deflation — quick revision

**Definitions (precision scores marks)**
- Inflation: **sustained** rise in the **general** price level. Deflation: sustained fall. Disinflation: inflation *rate* falls (prices still rise).

**CPI method**
1. Expenditure survey → basket. 2. **Weights** by spending share. 3. Monthly price collection. 4. Index; inflation = % change.
- Limits: basket outdated; quality changes; national average ≠ any one household.

**Causes**
- **Demand-pull**: AD shifts right near full capacity (credit booms, fiscal stimulus, export surges).
- **Cost-push**: SRAS shifts left (oil/raw materials, wages > productivity, currency depreciation, indirect taxes) → prices ↑ output ↓.
- **Monetarist**: money supply grows faster than output → hyperinflation if extreme (Zimbabwe, Venezuela).

**Consequences**
- Fixed incomes & savers lose; borrowers gain (real debt shrinks).
- Competitiveness ↓ → current account worsens.
- Menu & shoe-leather costs; uncertainty → investment ↓; fiscal drag.
- ~2% stable inflation is healthy — the target is low, not zero.

**Deflation**
- Postponed spending → AD ↓ → wages/jobs cut → spiral; real debt burden rises (Japan's lost decades).
- Benign exception: cost-cutting technology.

**Exam habit**
- Diagnose the type from data clues (what happened to output? costs? the currency?) — the policy answer follows.
questions:
# Practice questions — Inflation & deflation **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** A country's inflation rate falls from 9% to 4%. This is an example of: - A — deflation - B — disinflation - C — reflation - D — stagflation
▸ Show answer

B. The price level is still rising (4%), just more slowly — disinflation. Deflation would need the price level itself to fall (a negative rate).

**2.** Items in the CPI basket are weighted in order to: - A — remove the effect of inflation - B — reflect their importance in typical household spending - C — keep the index equal to 100 - D — exclude luxury goods
▸ Show answer

B. A price rise in something households spend heavily on (rent, food) should move the index more than one in a trivial item — weights achieve this.

**3.** Which of the following is most likely to cause cost-push inflation? - A — a rise in consumer confidence - B — an increase in government spending - C — a depreciation of the currency raising import prices - D — a cut in income tax
▸ Show answer

C. Dearer imported materials and goods raise firms' costs → SRAS shifts left. A, B and D all work through higher spending — demand-pull.

**4.** Unanticipated inflation is most likely to benefit: - A — a pensioner on a fixed income - B — a saver holding cash deposits - C — a household with a large fixed-interest mortgage - D — a worker whose wages are fixed by a three-year contract
▸ Show answer

C. Inflation erodes the real value of the debt while the repayments stay fixed — borrowers gain; the other three all see the real value of their income or savings fall.

## Paper 2 style — data response > After its currency depreciates by 25%, a small import-dependent economy sees CPI inflation rise from 3% to 11% within a year. Wage settlements accelerate as unions try to protect living standards, and several firms report cutting production because of higher energy and materials costs. The statistics office notes that food and fuel make up 40% of the CPI basket weights. **(a)** Define 'inflation'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain why food and fuel carry such large weights in this country's CPI. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse, with the aid of an AD/AS diagram, whether the inflation described is demand-pull or cost-push. **[6]** **(d)** Assess who loses most — and whether anyone gains — from this episode of inflation. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) A sustained rise in the general price level / sustained fall in the purchasing power of money. [1+1]

(b) Weights mirror household spending shares; in lower-income economies food and fuel dominate budgets, so their prices must dominate the index for it to reflect the cost of living. [4]

(c) Evidence points to cost-push: depreciation → import prices up; energy/materials costs up; output falling while prices rise (SRAS left). Wage acceleration risks a wage–price spiral sustaining it. Diagram: SRAS1 → SRAS2, P↑ Y↓. [6]

(d) Biggest losers: fixed-income households and the poor (food/fuel = 40% of spending), savers, firms dependent on imports. Relative gainers: borrowers (real debt falls), exporters (weaker currency), government debt in real terms. Judgement on severity given the distribution of spending. [8]

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain how a consumer price index is constructed and used to measure inflation. **[8]** **(b)** Assess the difficulties of relying on the CPI to judge changes in the cost of living. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Survey → basket → weights → monthly prices → weighted index → % change = inflation rate; a small worked example helps.

(b) Outdated basket and quality change (new phones better and dearer); average masks pensioners/students/regional differences; substitution by consumers when relative prices change; methodology revisions. Judgement: good guide to the trend, imperfect for any individual household.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain, with diagrams, the difference between demand-pull and cost-push inflation. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether deflation is a greater danger to an economy than inflation. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) AD right along steep AS (demand-pull, output up) vs SRAS left (cost-push, output down); one clear cause each.

(b) Deflation: postponed spending, deflationary spiral, rising real debt, monetary policy weakens near zero rates (Japan). Inflation: redistributes arbitrarily, uncertainty, competitiveness, hyperinflation risk if unanchored. Judgement: mild inflation is manageable and targeted (~2%); entrenched deflation is harder to escape — but hyperinflation is the worst outcome of all. Context decides.
